title: Exact versus tight-binding models in longitudinally modulated $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric coupled waveguides
abstract: The tight-binding (TB) model is a widely adopted approximation scheme for describing light propagation in waveguide arrays. Despite its success, its validity in $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric systems characterized by strong longitudinal modulation has not been rigorously benchmarked against exact analytical solutions. In this work, we address this gap by performing a comparative analysis between exact continuous solutions derived from $z$-dependent supersymmetric (SUSY) transformations and their corresponding discrete TB approximations. To achieve this, we develop a theoretical model for two PT-symmetric coupled waveguides subject to longitudinal modulation. We then evaluate the performance of the TB framework against the exact SUSY benchmark. Our results delineate the specific validity range of the TB approximation, demonstrating its proficiency in reproducing spatial intensity distributions. However, we also identify its limitations in accurately capturing the complex oscillatory phase dynamics inherent to this non-Hermitian evolution.
summary: This paper investigates how well the widely used tight-binding approximation describes light propagation in PT-symmetric waveguides with longitudinal modulation. While the approximation is reliable for tracking spatial trajectories, it fails to accurately predict phase-sensitive quantities like power oscillations and spectral fluctuations. This provides a critical warning for using discrete models to study complex non-Hermitian evolution.
main_problem: Evaluating the validity and accuracy of the tight-binding (TB) approximation compared to exact analytical solutions in PT-symmetric coupled waveguide arrays, particularly under longitudinal modulation.
main_result: The TB model accurately reproduces spatial dynamics like position and beam width but fails to capture complex phase dynamics, exhibiting significant phase shifts in total power and inaccuracies in spectral broadening.
method: The authors use second-order supersymmetric (Darboux) transformations to derive exact analytical solutions and compare them against a discrete tight-binding framework calibrated via a spectral matching method.
model_or_system: A system of two PT-symmetric coupled optical waveguides with longitudinal modulation, modeled by a non-Hermitian paraxial wave equation with a complex, z-dependent refractive index profile.
key_observables: Position expectation value, transverse momentum, spatial uncertainty (delta x, delta p), total power, and Hamiltonian expectation values (real and imaginary parts) using the PT inner product.
important_parameters: Longitudinal modulation strength, gain/loss parameter (alpha), coupling strength (kappa), and wave-number parameters (k1, k2, k3).
main_assumptions: The TB approximation assumes evolution is governed by discrete hopping between localized modes and is expected to converge as waveguide separation increases.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares Hermitian evolution of spatial and momentum observables; Figure 3 compares PT-symmetric evolution, highlighting power phase shifts; Figure 4 and 6 analyze Hamiltonian expectation values and fluctuations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of TB validity, develops exact potentials using SUSY transformations, implements the TB approximation and calibration, and performs a comparative analysis of spatial and energetic dynamics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it benchmarks a common approximation scheme used to study non-Hermitian dynamics and synthetic quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Real and imaginary parts of the propagation-distance-dependent PT -symmetric potential VS(x, z). Panels (a) and (b) depict transversal profiles associated with the limiting cases of the system: the strictly Hermitian potential (α = 0) and the propagation-distance-independent PT -symmetric potential (k3 →k1) (Re[V ] in black solid line and Im[V ] in red dashed line), respectively. Panels (c) and (d) show the full three-dimensional Re[V ] and Im[V ] over two spatial periods TV along the propagation axis z, showing the periodic modulation of the coupled waveguides and their balanced gain and loss. Parameters: k1 = 1, k2 = 1.1, k3 = 0.95, and α = 0.5.

FIG. 2. Evolution of the observables for the guided mode ψl, comparing the exact solution (blue solid line) with the tight-binding model (red dashed line). Panels show: (a) position expectation value, (b) momentum expectation value, (c) position standard deviation, and (d) momentum standard deviation.

FIG. 3. Dynamical evolution of the guided mode ψl for the exact (blue solid line) and approximated (red dashed line) models. Panels show the expectation values of (a) position, ⟨x⟩, (b) transverse momentum, ⟨p⟩, and (c) total power, P. Note the near-antiphase behavior of the power between the exact solution and the approximation. Panels (d) and (e) display the standard deviations of position, ∆x, and momentum, ∆p, respectively. These plots are generated using the localized states defined in Eq. (30).

FIG. 4. Analysis of the expectation value of the Hamiltonian for the exact (blue solid line) and approximated (red dashed line) guided mode ψl. Panels show the (a) real and (b) imaginary components of the Hamiltonian expectation value, ⟨H⟩, calculated using the standard Hermitian inner product. Panels (c) and (d) display the real and imaginary components of the Hamiltonian uncertainty, ∆H, respectively.

FIG. 5. Dynamical evolution of the guided mode ψl for the exact (blue solid line) and approximated (red dashed line) solutions. Panels show the expectation values of (a) position, ⟨x⟩, and (b) transverse momentum, ⟨p⟩, along with (c) the total power, P. Panels (d) and (e) display the standard deviations of position, ∆x, and momentum, ∆p, respectively.
